title: The Category Shell Game: Why Naming a Market No Longer Means Owning It

source.original: The Next Biggest Moat in AI — Jaya Gupta

body_html:
Every few months, a new term sweeps through AI: context graph, system of action, organizational world model. A category gets named, every startup's website absorbs it, and within weeks the market is saturated with companies claiming to be the inevitable platform for how work will change. The naming itself has become trivially easy, and with it, the strategic value of category creation has collapsed.

This is a fundamental shift from previous eras of tech, where defining a category—cloud, SaaS, social—could grant a company years of structural advantage. Today, when models improve rapidly, interfaces converge, and product velocity becomes cheap, the visible parts of company-building are imitable almost immediately. Infrastructure companies move up into workflows, application-layer startups collapse into infrastructure, and competitors that once seemed worlds apart suddenly share the same positioning deck.

The implication is that founders who believe they can win by naming something new are playing a game that no longer compounds. The words change every cycle, but the underlying question remains: what exists beneath the category label that cannot be replicated by the next startup to adopt the same language? The answer is almost never the product surface or the pitch vocabulary.

What endures is the institution underneath—the specific way a company attracts exceptional people, organizes their ambition, distributes authority, and turns individual judgment into a compounding system. The moat has migrated from the thing you call yourself to the thing you actually are: a human structure that no rebrand can reproduce.

Founders chasing category ownership are optimizing for a window that is now measured in weeks. The companies that will matter are the ones building organizational shapes so specific and so difficult to imitate that no amount of website copy can close the gap.
